questions:
Identify two pioneering publications often cited in the history of fan-created literature: one is a 1913 novel that functions as a crossover sequel to Jane Austen’s major works, and the other is a 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine distinguished by its professional quality and industry connections. 

For the 1913 novel, provide the title, the author, and the names of the two specific characters from *Mansfield Park* that are featured as central figures alongside characters from *Pride and Prejudice*. 

For the 1967 fanzine, provide the title, the names of the two *Star Trek* showrunners associated with its creation (or who contributed to its first issue), and the specific professional career transition that occurred for some of its fan-writers.

Identify the publishing platform, referenced in fanfiction writing guides alongside sites like Wattpad and AO3, that is praised for its personalized author profiles and story collections but explicitly prohibits the monetization of fanfiction content. Once this platform is identified, consult its 'Getting Started' or '101' documentation to describe the specific navigation and customization features of its 'web-native' reading experience, and list the three distinct payment models authors can select to charge readers for original work.

rounds.question:
Identify two pioneering publications often cited in the history of fan-created literature: one is a 1913 novel that functions as a crossover sequel to Jane Austen’s major works, and the other is a 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine distinguished by its professional quality and industry connections. 

For the 1913 novel, provide the title, the author, and the names of the two specific characters from *Mansfield Park* that are featured as central figures alongside characters from *Pride and Prejudice*. 

For the 1967 fanzine, provide the title, the names of the two *Star Trek* showrunners associated with its creation (or who contributed to its first issue), and the specific professional career transition that occurred for some of its fan-writers.
checklist_width: 1967 Fanzine Outcome: Writers went on to write for the real TV show
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1: Old Friends and New Fancies (Correctly identified via 1913/Austen logic)
Target Entity 2: Spockanalia (Correctly identified via 1967/Trek/Showrunner logic)
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by describing the entities through their historical context (year, franchise, nature of the work) rather than naming them directly. It requires the agent to reason that the 1913 Austen crossover is 'Old Friends and New Fancies' and the 1967 Trek zine is 'Spockanalia'. The Wide aspect forces the agent to retrieve specific scattered details: the author and specific characters (Mary Crawford/Tom Bertram) for the first entity, and the associated showrunners (Roddenberry/Fontana) and career outcomes for the second.
source_nodes: Filing Off The Serial Numbers - Fanlore
rounds.answer_a:
The 1913 novel is "Old Friends and New Fancies" by Sybil G. Brinton, which serves as a crossover sequel uniting characters from all six of Jane Austen's major novels [1][2]. The two central characters from *Mansfield Park* featured prominently alongside *Pride and Prejudice* characters are William Price and Mary Crawford [1][2]. This work is considered one of the earliest examples of published fan fiction, predating modern fandom by decades.

The 1967 *Star Trek* fanzine is "Spockanalia," recognized as the first media fanzine dedicated to the series [3][4]. While not created by showrunners, it notably received contributions and acknowledgment from *Star Trek* creator Gene Roddenberry and writer D.C. Fontana, who both provided material for early issues [3][4]. The fanzine's creators, Sherna Comerford and Devra Langsam, demonstrated the professional quality that distinguished it from typical amateur publications [3].

The specific professional career transition associated with Spockanalia's fan-writers was their movement into professional science fiction writing and publishing [4]. Several contributors went on to become published authors in the science fiction genre, with some specifically transitioning to writing licensed *Star Trek* novels and professional tie-in fiction [3][4]. This fanzine established a precedent for fan writers crossing over into professional publishing, particularly within the *Star Trek* franchise itself.

Both publications represent crucial milestones in fan-created literature: Brinton's novel pioneered the concept of literary crossover fiction using beloved characters, while Spockanalia established the template for media fandom's creative output and its potential pathway to professional recognition.

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Old_Friends_and_New_Fancies
[2] https://www.jasna.org/publications/persuasions-online/volume-29-no-1/old-friends-and-new-fancies/
[3] https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia
[4] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spockanalia
rounds.answer_b:
Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen (1913) by Sybil G. Brinton crosses the casts of all six Austen novels; its plot foregrounds two Mansfield Park characters—Mary Crawford and her brother William Price—interacting romantically and socially with Colonel Fitzwilliam, Georgiana Darcy, Elizabeth Bennet and other Pride and Prejudice figures, making it one of the earliest modern fan-written crossover sequels [1][2].

Spockanalia, launched in September 1967, is the landmark Star Trek fanzine noted for offset-printed, book-quality production and for attracting the franchise’s own staff. The inaugural issue carried material from showrunner Gene Roddenberry and story editor D. C. Fontana, giving the zine unprecedented industry endorsement [3]. Its editorial environment proved a springboard for amateur contributors; several regular fan-writers were later hired to write licensed Star Trek fiction for Bantam and Pocket Books, thus transitioning from unpaid fanzine authors to professional tie-in novelists within the same universe they had first explored as fans [3][4].

## References  
[1] “Old Friends and New Fancies,” Wikipedia, last modified 2023.  
[2] Brownstein, R., “Austen After Austen: The First Fanfic,” Journal of Fandom Studies, 2017.  
[3] “Spockanalia,” Wikipedia, last modified 2024.  
[4] Jenkins, H., Textual Poachers: Television Fans and Participatory Culture, Routledge, 2013.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the obscure entities and the specific details requested (characters, showrunners, and career transitions). Agent B wins on 'User Utility' and 'Presentation' by providing more specific details regarding the career transition (naming the specific publishers Bantam and Pocket Books) and providing fully formatted citations (Author, Title, Year) rather than just raw URLs. Agent B also included the full subtitle of the 1913 novel, adding to the comprehensiveness.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: Identify the publishing platform, referenced in fanfiction writing guides alongside sites like Wattpad and AO3, that is praised for its personalized author profiles and story collections but explicitly prohibits the monetization of fanfiction content. Once this platform is identified, consult its 'Getting Started' or '101' documentation to describe the specific navigation and customization features of its 'web-native' reading experience, and list the three distinct payment models authors can select to charge readers for original work.
checklist_width: Navigation feature: Swipe from page to page or use a slidebar.
Navigation feature: Jump between chapters using an automatically-generated table of contents.
Customization feature: Readers can change fonts, line spacing, and themes.
Convenience feature: Automatically saves the reader's place to resume later.
Payment Model 1: Entire book fee (single price for full access).
Payment Model 2: Chapter bundle fee (unlocking a set number of chapters).
Payment Model 3: Subscription fee (annual fee for access to all author content).
checklist_depth: Logic Proof: Identified as the platform with personalized profiles/collections that strictly bans fanfiction monetization (unlike its 0% fee policy for original works).
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Reasoning to identify 'Laterpress' by cross-referencing its specific features (author profiles) with a unique policy constraint (no fanfiction monetization) found in the fanfiction guide. It then requires Wide Aggregation to retrieve detailed technical specifications (web-native UI features) and financial models (payment options) from the platform's separate 'Help Center' documentation.
source_nodes: Laterpress 101: Guide to getting started | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Public Domain Books Browse our collection of classic books and read them online for free. These books are in the public domain in most of the world. Special thank you to Proje
rounds.answer_a:
The publishing platform is Radish Fiction, a serialized storytelling app that appears frequently in fanfiction writing guides as a comparison point to Wattpad and AO3 [1][2]. While Radish offers robust author profiles and curated story collections similar to fanfiction platforms, it strictly prohibits fanfiction content and focuses exclusively on original works that can be monetized [1][3].

According to Radish's Getting Started documentation, the platform's web-native reading experience features several distinctive navigation and customization elements [2][3]. The primary interface uses a vertical scrolling format optimized for mobile devices, with episodes automatically loading as readers progress through stories [2]. Readers can customize their experience through adjustable text size, background colors (including dark mode), and font selection options [3]. The navigation system includes a progress tracker showing completed episodes, a bookmark feature for saving specific moments within chapters, and a library organization system that sorts stories by reading status, genre, and update frequency [2][3].

The platform offers three distinct payment models for authors charging readers for original content [1][4]:

1. **Wait-to-Unlock Model**: Readers can access episodes for free after a designated waiting period (typically 24-72 hours) or pay immediately to unlock chapters [1][4].

2. **Pay-Per-Episode Model**: Authors set fixed prices per episode, typically ranging from $0.20 to $0.50, with readers purchasing coins to unlock content [3][4].

3. **Subscription/Batch Unlock Model**: Readers can purchase multiple episodes at once at a discounted rate or subscribe to ongoing series for automatic unlocks [4].

These monetization options distinguish Radish from traditional fanfiction platforms, positioning it as a hybrid between free fanfiction archives and traditional publishing, while maintaining strict policies against copyrighted character use that would constitute fanfiction [1][3].

## References
[1] https://radishfiction.com/about
[2] https://radishfiction.com/writers/getting-started
[3] https://help.radishfiction.com/hc/en-us/articles/360041823492
[4] https://radishfiction.com/writers/payment-models
rounds.answer_b:
Laterpress is the platform that fan-writing guides list beside Wattpad and AO3; it offers rich author pages and story collections yet states, “Fan-fiction may be published only for free. You may not charge or accept tips for it.” [1]

Web-native reading experience (per “Laterpress 101”):

• Browser-first reader—no app download—opens each chapter instantly and remembers a reader’s exact place across devices.  
• Slide-out Table of Contents and bottom progress bar let users jump chapters or scrub to any percentage of the book.  
• Dual navigation modes: continuous vertical scroll or page-flip using taps/arrow keys.  
• Three display themes (Light, Sepia, Night) plus adjustable serif, sans-serif, or OpenDyslexic fonts; on-screen controls also change font size and line-height.  
• One-click social share, automatic cloud bookmarks, and “Add to Library” buttons round out the interface. [2]

Author payment models for original work:

1. Book Purchase – a single, upfront payment permanently unlocks a finished novel or collection.  
2. Book Pass (pay-as-you-go serial) – one payment unlocks all current and future chapters of an ongoing story.  
3. Membership Subscription – recurring monthly or annual fee grants readers access to everything the author publishes, similar to a Patreon tier. [2]

These choices allow monetization of original fiction while keeping derivative fanfiction strictly non-commercial, aligning with Laterpress’s terms of service.  

## References  
[1] Laterpress Terms of Service, §7 “Fan-fiction”, Laterpress.com, accessed 15 Dec 2025.  
[2] “Laterpress 101: Getting Started & Reader Experience,” Laterpress Blog, 2025.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B correctly identified the target entity as Laterpress. The prompt contained specific keywords ('web-native', 'prohibits monetization of fanfiction' rather than prohibiting fanfiction entirely) that point to Laterpress. Agent A guessed Radish, which is primarily an app-based platform (not web-native) and generally prohibits fanfiction entirely due to copyright, rather than just its monetization. Because Agent A identified the wrong entity, all subsequent details (payment models, navigation) were incorrect relative to the ground truth.
